title: Exploring extreme parameter compression for pre-trained language models

structured.summary: The paper explores using Tucker decomposition for extreme parameter compression in pre-trained language models (PLMs) to reduce computational costs and facilitate easier deployment of models like BERT. Despite achieving notable compression ratios and faster inference times, the paper's contributions are less novel and lack detailed evaluation, reproducibility, and comparative analysis with other compression methods.

strengths.evidence: Compressed BERT with 1/7 parameters performs on-par with the original BERT; a version with 1/48 parameters achieves 96.7% performance with 2.7x faster inference.
The paper proposes a formal framework to discuss matrix/tensor decomposition for compressing language models.

weaknesses.point: Lack of Novelty in Decomposition Techniques
weaknesses.evidence: Application of Tucker decomposition is not novel; similar techniques have been applied to compress CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers before, reducing the methodological impact.
Specifics about the experimental setup, such as datasets used beyond the GLUE benchmark, and detailed configurations are missing (Section 3).
Claims about performance maintenance or improvement on GLUE benchmark are vague, lacking specificity about individual tasks (Abstract, Section 1).
No qualitative evaluation or analysis related to practical, real-world application of the compressed models is provided (Sections 3 and 4).
Improvements in efficiency and speedup lack detailed computational complexity analysis of the decomposition and reconstruction protocols (Section 3).

weaknesses.point: Limited Discussion on Comparative Compression Limits
weaknesses.evidence: Does not sufficiently compare the compression limits of the approach with those of existing compression methods like quantization and pruning (Related Work section).
Claims of compatibility with knowledge distillation lack empirical evidence or detailed demonstration (Abstract, Section 3).
Critical details such as hyperparameters, training schedules, and hardware specifications are not clearly included, hindering reproducibility (Section 3).
The abstract lacks precise statistics or comparisons to fully substantiate the claims about success of the proposed method.

weaknesses.point: Ignoring Potential Drawbacks of Tucker Decomposition
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not address potential downsides like model interpretability or impact on convergence time (Main Sections).

questions.question: How does the proposed method compare with existing compression methods in terms of real-world application scenarios?
questions.motivation: Understanding practical applicability beyond benchmarks is essential for assessing the method's utility.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Decomposition Techniques. Application of Tucker decomposition is not novel; similar techniques have been applied to compress CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers before, reducing the methodological impact
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient Evaluation Details. Specifics about the experimental setup, such as datasets used beyond the GLUE benchmark, and detailed configurations are missing (Section 3)
critique_points.point_003: Ambiguity in Performance Metrics. Claims about performance maintenance or improvement on GLUE benchmark are vague, lacking specificity about individual tasks (Abstract, Section 1)
critique_points.point_004: Lack of Qualitative Experimentation. No qualitative evaluation or analysis related to practical, real-world application of the compressed models is provided (Sections 3 and 4)
critique_points.point_005: Overlooked Computational Costs in Decomposition. Improvements in efficiency and speedup lack detailed computational complexity analysis of the decomposition and reconstruction protocols (Section 3)
critique_points.point_006: Limited Discussion on Comparative Compression Limits. Does not sufficiently compare the compression limits of the approach with those of existing compression methods like quantization and pruning (Related Work section)
critique_points.point_007: Inconsistency in Claims on Complementarity. Claims of compatibility with knowledge distillation lack empirical evidence or detailed demonstration (Abstract, Section 3)
critique_points.point_008: Lack of Reproducibility Details. Critical details such as hyperparameters, training schedules, and hardware specifications are not clearly included, hindering reproducibility (Section 3)
critique_points.point_009: Abstract Lacks Specificity. The abstract lacks precise statistics or comparisons to fully substantiate the claims about success of the proposed method
critique_points.point_010: Ignoring Potential Drawbacks of Tucker Decomposition. The paper does not address potential downsides like model interpretability or impact on convergence time (Main Sections)
